title: Tool Bloat & Attention Mismatch
concept: Why giving an agent too many tools reduces accuracy, and how to group or scope toolsets.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: which item belongs on a good tool optimization checklist?

options.text: Clear docstrings so the agent knows when to use the tool
Make every tool's name a random string of numbers
Remove all timeouts so tools can run forever

exercises.explain: A solid checklist includes typed schemas, detailed docstrings, active validation, timeouts, and sandboxed side-effects. Clear docstrings help the agent pick the right tool at the right moment.

cards.title: More Isn't Always Better
cards.body: Imagine a phone with 500 apps on one screen. Finding the right one gets harder, not easier. Agents feel the same overwhelm when handed too many tools.

cards.title: What Is Tool Bloat?
cards.body: Tool bloat is when an agent has so many tools that it struggles to pick the right one. Its attention gets spread thin, so accuracy drops even if each tool works fine on its own.

cards.title: The Overstuffed Toolbox
cards.body: A toolbox with 3 clearly labeled tools is easy to grab from. A toolbox with 200 tangled tools makes you fumble and pick the wrong one. Agents fumble the same way.

cards.title: Scoping Fixes It
cards.body: A travel agent given only flight, hotel, and weather tools books trips reliably. Add 60 unrelated tools like 'edit_photo' and 'run_payroll', and it starts calling the wrong ones. Grouping tools by task keeps it sharp.

cards.title: Myth: Give It Everything
cards.body: People think loading every possible tool makes an agent more capable. Actually it confuses the agent's attention and lowers accuracy. Fewer, well-scoped tools usually win.

cards.title: Bloat vs. Scoped
cards.body: Match the tools to the task instead of dumping them all in at once.

left.text: 80 tools at once, agent picks wrong ones, accuracy drops

right.text: 6 relevant tools, agent picks confidently, accuracy stays high

exercises.prompt: Why does giving an agent too many tools often reduce accuracy?

options.text: The extra tools break the internet connection
Its attention spreads thin, so it picks the wrong tool more often
Tools automatically delete each other when there are too many
The agent gets bored and stops working

exercises.explain: Each extra tool is one more option the agent must consider. With too many choices, its attention is split and it's more likely to grab the wrong tool. Scoping the toolset keeps decisions clear.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Grouping tools by task can help an agent choose the right one.
exercises.explain: True. Grouping or scoping tools to the current task means the agent only sees relevant options, which reduces confusion and improves accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about fixing tool bloat.
exercises.sentence: To reduce tool bloat, you should scope the toolset so the agent only sees tools relevant to the current task.
exercises.explain: You scope the toolset by showing only the tools that fit the current task. This shrinks the choices and keeps the agent's attention focused.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of tool bloat sounds AI-generated (smooth and textbook-perfect)?

options.text: Tool bloat occurs when an excess of available tools dilutes an agent's attention, thereby degrading its selection accuracy across tasks.
too many tools = agent gets confused lol picks wrong one
My agent had like 90 tools and kept grabbing the payroll one for travel, so annoying

exercises.explain: The first option is the AI-generated one: polished, formal, and evenly structured. The others sound casual and human, with slang and personal frustration.

exercises.prompt: Your customer-support agent has 70 tools and keeps calling the wrong ones, lowering accuracy. What's the best fix?

options.text: Add even more tools so it always has an option
Group tools by task and expose only the relevant scoped set for each situation
Remove all tools so it can't make mistakes
Rename every tool with random numbers

exercises.explain: The winning move is to group tools by task and scope them, so the agent only sees the handful relevant to the moment. This narrows its attention and boosts accuracy without losing capability. More tools cause bloat; zero tools cause helplessness; scoped grouping hits the sweet spot.
